title: Guacamole
subtitle: Avocado mashed with lime, coriander, and chilli.
excerpt: Ripe avocados crushed coarsely with lime juice, chopped white onion, fresh coriander, and a finely chopped green chilli. The Mexican household dip, made fresh and eaten within the hour.
sourceNotes: The dish dates to pre-Columbian Aztec cookery, with ahuacatl (avocado) mashed with chilli and salt. The classical Mexican mortar-and-pestle method is documented in Diana Kennedy's research on regional Mexican cuisine.

content.text: Four ripe Hass avocados, the juice of a lime, half a small white onion chopped fine, a small handful of coriander, one or two green chillies, salt. Crushed in a bowl with the back of a fork until the texture is roughly half-smooth and half-chunky. The whole thing takes ten minutes.
Ripeness is the only thing that matters. An avocado that yields gently to a thumb-press at the stem end is right. Anything firmer is unripe; anything bruised is past it.

items.name: Onion
items.prepNote: small white onion, very finely chopped

items.name: Green chilli
items.prepNote: deseeded if you want it mild, finely chopped

content.text: A molcajete gives the classical coarse texture, but a medium bowl and the back of a fork works as well. A sharp knife matters: bruised tomato and ragged onion will spoil the texture.

Tip {{onion}} of finely chopped onion into a bowl with {{chilli-green}} of chopped chilli, {{sea-salt-flakes}} of salt, and half the {{coriander}} of chopped coriander. Press with the back of a wooden spoon for a minute; this bruises the onion and chilli enough to release the oils and softens the raw bite.

Scoop the flesh of {{avocado}} of avocados into the bowl with a spoon. Squeeze in the juice of {{lime}} of lime. Mash with a fork until about half the flesh is smooth and half is still in lumps the size of a fingernail. Don't overdo it; a smooth purée goes brown faster and reads more like a dip than a salad.

Stir in the remaining coriander and the chopped tomato if using. Taste and add more salt or lime as needed. Tip into a serving bowl and eat within the hour with warm tortillas, tortilla chips, or alongside chicken fajitas.

items.fix: Wait a day or two. An avocado that yields to a gentle thumb-press at the stem end is ready. If the kitchen is cool, ripening can take 3 days; in a paper bag with a banana, faster.
Aim for half smooth, half chunky. Stop the moment most of the avocado is broken down but lumps remain. A blender or food processor is wrong for this dish.
Press cling film directly to the surface, or pour a thin layer of cold water on top until serving, pour off the water just before eating.
items.cause: Too long uncovered, or warm conditions.

items.fix: Cut around any brown patches on the avocado. Use leaves only, the stems are bitter when chopped fine.
items.cause: Avocado past prime, or coriander stems left in.

content.text: Guacamole travelled from Aztec kitchens to Spanish missionary records to the Tex-Mex Sunday plate. Its place at the centre of a snack table (alongside salsa, chips, and a cold drink) is fixed. The Mexican household version is simpler than the loaded American take, with no garlic, no cream, no extra fat. The avocado does the work.

recipe.yieldDescription: ~400 g, enough for a generous side dip
